summary: Dental fractures range from minor enamel cracks to vertical root fractures. Treatment depends on fracture type and extent: craze lines require monitoring, horizontal fractures may be splinted and stabilized, while vertical root fractures typically necessitate extraction. Crown fractures exposing dentin require bonding or crown restoration. Fractures involving pulp exposure need endodontic treatment followed by full-coverage restoration.
risk_factors: History of bruxism or teeth grinding
Large amalgam restorations creating stress concentrators
alternatives: Composite bonding for minor enamel-dentin fractures
Full-coverage crown for significant coronal fractures
Root canal therapy with post-core and crown if pulp is involved
Extraction with implant replacement for vertical root fractures
referral_summary: General dentists manage most coronal fractures. Endodontist referral is needed for complicated crown fractures with pulp exposure or horizontal root fractures requiring splinting. Oral surgery referral for extraction of vertically fractured teeth and implant planning.
patient_education: A tooth fracture is a crack or break in your tooth. Small cracks in the enamel are usually harmless and just need monitoring. Deeper fractures that reach the inner part of the tooth may cause sensitivity to hot, cold, or biting pressure. Depending on the severity, treatment can range from a simple bonding to a crown or root canal. If you notice sharp pain when biting or releasing, let your dentist know right away.
